planting.note: Plant dormant bare-root canes in early spring before bud break, or divisions in fall; black raspberry tolerates partial shade better than red raspberry, making it valuable for forest edges and lightly shaded food gardens. The second-year canes (floricanes) bear fruit, so new plantings fruit in their second year.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil; black raspberry is a native eastern North American cane fruit with intensely flavored, deep purple-black fruits that are prized above all other raspberries for their rich, complex flavor — more aromatic and concentrated than red or purple types. The canes are biennial, growing vegetatively the first year (primocanes) and fruiting in summer of the second year (floricanes), after which they die and should be cut out. New primocanes arise from the base annually. Unlike red raspberry, black raspberry spreads by tip-rooting: when the arching cane tips touch the ground in late summer, they root and form new plants. Harvest the hollow-centered fruits when they turn fully black — they release easily from the core. Excellent fresh, in preserves, syrup, and wine; also dried.
sources: Cornell Small Farms Program — Small Fruit Crop Guides
Strik and Finn: Blackberry and Raspberry Growing in Oregon, OSU Extension 2015

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring or fall; mock strawberry is extremely adaptable and will colonize partial to full shade under trees where few groundcovers thrive. It spreads by runners similar to regular strawberry and can cover large areas quickly.

directions: Plant in partial to full shade in moist, well-drained to moderately moist, average soil — mock strawberry is one of the most shade-tolerant edible groundcovers available for difficult spots under trees. It spreads vigorously by runners, forming a dense weed-suppressing carpet of trifoliate leaves studded with small yellow flowers from spring through fall followed by round, bright red berries. The fruits are edible and safe but mild and nearly tasteless compared to true strawberries — they lack the complex aroma that makes true strawberry irresistible. They are best used in jams or flavored beverages rather than eaten fresh. The plant does well in lawn edges, woodland paths, and shaded slopes where it outcompetes many weeds while providing interest through yellow flowers and bright fruit. May naturalize aggressively; divide to control spread.

planting.note: Plant rooted cane tips or divisions in early spring; dewberry is the trailing, low-growing, first-ripening blackberry type native to eastern North America — it thrives in partial shade, open woodland edges, and dry, sandy soils that challenge cultivated blackberries.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, acidic soil; northern dewberry is a trailing native blackberry that runs along the ground rather than forming erect canes, with individual canes spreading 3–6 feet from the crown. It is among the first bramble fruits to ripen in summer — often a full month before other blackberries — producing glossy, deep black, juicy, intensely sweet-tart fruits excellent for fresh eating and preserves. The trailing habit makes it suitable for slopes, embankments, and naturalized woodland edges; it thrives under open forest canopy where cultivated blackberries would languish. Unlike erect blackberries it does not sucker aggressively but can spread by cane-tip rooting. The canes have sharp prickles; wear gloves for harvest and management.
sources: University of Maryland Extension Small Fruit Guides
